body_txt: A Sub-Trope of Parenthetical Swearing , this is a line of dialogue which may be used for Getting Crap Past the Radar , or that at least always leads to many raised eyebrows and jokes amongst the online fandom. Basically, Bob calls Alice a "witch." The context and inflection, however, strongly imply that, in a world free of Media Watchdogs and censors, he would have used a different epithet — one that rhymes with "witch" but starts with a B. Extra fun if it's justified with the subject being a literal witch or, conversely, the speaker being a female dog (who wouldn't use a word that means "female dog" as an insult). More adult-oriented works tend to do the opposite: wherever the word for a magic-using crone would suffice (" wicked witch ", "witchcraft"), they'll go for the swear. And this is not just in English...Spanish speakers may like to use "bruja" as a polite form of "puta." note It varies by country. In some Latin countries, calling a woman a "bruja" means a mean lady or even insulting her appearance. Yes, this includes "hijo de bruja." Be warned, however, calling a girl a "bruja" could imply that she is more "a woman of ill repute", whereas calling a girl a "witch" would simply suggest she is "not a very nice lady." In Italy, too, calling a woman a 'strega' (witch) is a way to call her a 'stronza' (asshole). Setting the censors aside, "witch" was heavily used as an insult for women during the early years of The Renaissance , before becoming mainly an insult for unscrupulous old women . In case, using "witch" as an insult is an early sign that someone could Never Mess with Granny . Most prevalent in Western Animation . See also This Is for Emphasis, Bitch! , which can be used to set up this trope. It's often used when bowdlerizing a work for broadcast, in places where people in the version seen in videos, movie theaters, or CDs do say "bitch".
